cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Welcome in, thanks for making the time today.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Let's run a quick SQL round, the kind of rapid technical screen you'd get for an SDET role.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: I'll ask a question, you give me your best answer, and I may push a little deeper here and there.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Nothing tricky, just the fundamentals done well.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Ready when you are.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: Sounds great, let's do it.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Let's start with a classic.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: What's the difference between WHERE and HAVING, and why does the difference exist at all?

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The direct answer is that WHERE filters individual rows before any grouping happens, and HAVING filters the groups after GROUP BY has collapsed them, which is why HAVING can use aggregates like COUNT or SUM and WHERE can't. The reason the difference exists is the logical execution order of a query, the database runs FROM and the joins first, then WHERE, then GROUP BY, then HAVING, and only then SELECT, ORDER BY, and LIMIT.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: So when WHERE runs, the aggregate values simply don't exist yet.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: A concrete example I use all the time, if I want customers with more than a thousand in paid orders, I filter status equals paid in WHERE first so I only aggregate the right rows, then group by customer, then use HAVING on the sum.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: And that execution order explains a common trap too, a column alias defined in SELECT isn't visible in WHERE, because WHERE runs before SELECT, but it does work in ORDER BY, which runs last.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Nice, and you got the execution order in there before I could ask.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Next one, walk me through join types, and specifically, how would you find customers who have never placed an order?

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: A join combines rows from two tables on a matching condition, and the type decides what happens to non-matching rows.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: INNER keeps only rows that match on both sides, LEFT keeps every left row and fills the right columns with NULL when there's no match, RIGHT is the mirror of that, FULL OUTER keeps everything from both sides, and CROSS gives every combination, which is usually an accident.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: For customers with no orders, I'd LEFT JOIN orders onto customers on the customer id, then filter where the order id IS NULL, because those NULL rows are exactly the customers nothing matched.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: NOT EXISTS with a subquery works just as well.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The trap I always call out is that if you filter the right table's columns in the WHERE clause, say where order status equals paid, you silently turn the LEFT JOIN back into an INNER JOIN, because the NULL rows fail that condition and get dropped.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The fix is to put that condition in the ON clause instead.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Good, and you already touched on NULL there, so let's stay on it.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: What makes NULL tricky in SQL, and how do you handle it?

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The core idea is that NULL means unknown, not zero and not an empty string, and that unknown spreads through everything it touches.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: So you can never compare with equals NULL, that comparison is always unknown and never true, you have to use IS NULL or IS NOT NULL.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: Any arithmetic with NULL is NULL, five plus NULL is just NULL.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: And aggregates quietly skip NULLs, so AVG on a column ignores the null rows, and COUNT of a column counts only non-null values while COUNT star counts every row, which can surprise you in a report.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: For handling it, COALESCE returns the first non-null argument, so I can fall back from nickname to name to a default label, and NULLIF is handy for guarding a divide by zero, dividing by NULLIF of count and zero returns NULL instead of crashing.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The nastiest trap is NOT IN against a subquery that returns a NULL, that query returns no rows at all, so I reach for NOT EXISTS instead.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: That NOT IN trap has bitten a lot of people.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Now, suppose I ask you for the latest order per customer, or the second highest salary.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: What's your tool for those?

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: Both of those are window function problems.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: A window function computes over a set of related rows without collapsing them the way GROUP BY does, which is exactly what you need when you want the whole row back, not just an aggregate.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: For latest order per customer, I take ROW_NUMBER over a window partitioned by customer id and ordered by created at descending, wrap that in a subquery or CTE, and keep the rows where the row number is one.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: For second highest salary, same shape, DENSE_RANK over salary descending and filter where the rank equals two.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The reason I pick each function deliberately is the tie behavior, ROW_NUMBER is always unique, RANK leaves gaps after ties, so you'd see one, one, three, and DENSE_RANK doesn't, one, one, two.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: For a salary question ties matter, so DENSE_RANK is the honest answer.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The trap is forgetting PARTITION BY, then you're numbering the entire table as one big window instead of per customer.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Solid, and the tie behavior is exactly the follow-up I was holding.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Let's shift to performance.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: What does an index actually do for you, and when does it stop helping?

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: An index is a sorted lookup structure, usually a B-tree, that lets the database find rows without scanning the whole table.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The trade is real though, reads on the indexed columns get faster, but every insert, update, and delete has to maintain the index, and it costs storage, so the guidance I give is to index what you filter, join, and sort on, not every column.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: A composite index on two columns follows the left prefix rule, it helps filters on the first column or on both, but not on the second column alone.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The classic way an index stops helping is wrapping the column in a function, filtering where the year function of a date equals a value kills index use, and the fix is rewriting it as a date range so the predicate is sargable.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: When I suspect a slow query, I don't guess, I run EXPLAIN or EXPLAIN ANALYZE and check whether it's doing an index seek or a full scan.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Good instinct going to the plan instead of guessing.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: One more on the database side, tell me about transactions and what ACID actually guarantees.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: A transaction groups statements so they succeed or fail as one unit, and ACID is the set of guarantees that makes that reliable.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: Atomicity means all statements commit or none do, a rollback leaves nothing changed.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: Consistency means constraints hold before and after.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: Isolation means concurrent transactions don't corrupt each other, and durability means once it's committed, it survives a crash.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The textbook example is a money transfer, begin a transaction, subtract a hundred from one account, add a hundred to the other, then commit, and if anything fails in between, the rollback means you never end up with money vanishing from one side only.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: The nuance worth adding is that isolation isn't one thing, it's a dial of levels trading consistency for concurrency, read uncommitted allows dirty reads, read committed is the common default, repeatable read stops non-repeatable reads, and serializable is strictest and stops phantoms.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: Knowing that dial exists matters when tests read data mid-transaction.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Nice bridge to my last question, because you're interviewing for an SDET role, not a DBA role.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: How do you actually use SQL in your testing work?

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: For me SQL is a verification tool, I assert against the database, not just against the UI or the API.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: So after an action in a test, I query the table directly to confirm the row was written with the right values, which catches bugs where the API returns success but persists the wrong thing.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: I also run data quality checks, grouping by email with HAVING count greater than one to catch duplicates, and the LEFT JOIN with IS NULL pattern to catch orphaned orders pointing at customers that don't exist, plus null checks on required fields and row count reconciliation between source and target.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: Beyond assertions, I use SQL for setup and teardown, seeding fixtures and cleaning state from inside the test framework.

cues.speaker: CANDIDATE
cues.text: And a real lesson learned, on an order history bug our test picked the latest row by max id while the app used the updated at timestamp, so backfilled records broke the assertion until we matched the query to the actual product rule.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Strong answers, that's the round.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: What I liked here, and what listeners should take away, is the structure, a direct answer first, then the why, and then a concrete example or a trap called out.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: That pattern is what makes an answer sound senior.

cues.speaker: INTERVIEWER
cues.text: Practice these until they flow, and I'll see you in the next round.
